1. No poverty.summary: No supporting evidence was found in the provided context to determine the proportion of the population living below the international poverty line. The context primarily discusses land use, carbon emissions, and socio-economic situations in the Panama Canal Watershed area, but does not provide information on poverty rates or population demographics. The documents provided focus on project activities, such as land preparation, planting, and thinning, as well as socio-economic impacts, but do not contain relevant data on poverty rates or demographic variations by sex, age, employment status, or geographic location. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

1. No poverty.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the proportion of population living below the national poverty line, by sex and age. Although the socio-economic situation of the area is mentioned as low-income subsistence and livestock farming (Document: GS+Project+Information+09.27.2017.pdf, Page: 5), and 65% of the PCW area is under livestock use (Document: PDD_ACP_CFS.pdf, Page: 21), these details are qualitative and do not provide explicit quantitative data. Additionally, the project has implemented welfare activities, including a nursery ran by the women of the community (Document: GS+Performance+Certification+Report+09.29.2016.pdf, Page: 50), which may have a positive impact on the socio-economic situation of women, but this is also qualitative. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

W f)   Risk of the project area to change during the crediting period – The project proponent contracts companies for 5 years  at a time in order to ensure proper management and maintenance of the project areas.  Further, the agreements signed  with local landowners stipulate the same.    g)  Risk  of  the  project  activities  to  change  during  the  crediting  period  –  The  project  activities  are  continually  being  implemented and the project intends to expand the project and planting areas for future certification.  h)  Timeframe for the project activities – The project will last 30 years.  i)   Number of predicted CO2 certificates – The project estimates that there are 132,246 tCO2e of verifiable CO2 certificates  at the time of the 2016 Performance Certification.  j)   Land-use history and current situation of the project area – Slash and burn agriculture, followed by cattle grazing, has  dominated the landscape.  These land use practices continue outside of the project intervention.  k)  Socio-economic history and current situation – The socio-economic context of the project area is comprised of poor rural  subsistence farmers.  l)   Forest management applied (past and future) – Past forest management has been a process of continual degradation  from forested to agricultural and pasture lands.  Current forest management under the project is fomenting sustainable  land and forest management with the intent of increasing environmental services and reducing CO2 emissions.  m)   Forest characteristics – Neither the pre-project land use nor the baseline scenario involve forest management, as the  pre-existing  forests  are  no  more.    The  project  describes  the  incorporation  of  various  tree  species  in  to  the  project  landscape,  principally  depending  on  native  species  in  the  agroforestry  and  conservation  planting  areas,  and  teak/eucalyptus in the commercial areas.  n)  Main social impacts – The project counts on an improvement of environmental services for the surrounding communities  and  the  whole  of  the  Panama  Canal  Zone  and  an  increase  in  coffee-driven  economic  improvement  to  be  the  main  social impacts of the project. Feedback project developer (09 September 2013):  In the PDD the template was updated taken into account the following information:   Conservation forestry: conservation for 50 years   Commercial: rotation period of 25 years   Silvo-pasture: Selective; Frutal trees and services trees ( total 80%) are conservation for a period of 50 years  and the commercial trees ( 20%) will be logged after 25 years.   Agroforestry: Selective; trees and services trees ( total 80%) are conservation for a period of 50 years and the  commercial trees ( 20%) will be logged after 25 years.   iven  the  mix  of  species  involved  in  the  commercial  plantations,  with  currently  unknown  growth  rates,  the  developer  has  chosen  not  to  implement  the  specific  equation  under  “Option  2”  in  the  CarbonFix  Standard,  but   instead has opted to simply model the estimated carbon stock value at the end of the rotation period and reduce  this value by 50%.   The logic behind this is that it  is simpler and that the growth  model will be updated with  empirical data from the Management Units in the near future.  Conclusion of the certifier (27. September 2013):  The project developer has updated the PDD to provide more specific details about the harvesting plans for the  various management units and expected management plans.  These updates include specifically noting that the  commercial management units will be subject to rotation harvesting, thus addressing the nonconformity.    he audit team has evaluated what the estimated carbon stock value would be in the commercial management  units  using  the  “Option  2”  (time  average  carbon  stock)  equation  in  the  standard  and  compared  this  to  the  values   the developer has obtained by simply reducing the carbon stock at the end of the rotation period by 50%.  The  developer’s  values  are  slightly  more conservative.  Additionally, the values in the growth model will be updated | :

Evidence must be given that project activities will NOT lead to a long-term increase of greenhouse gas  emissions in the  carbon  pool  ‘soil’  on  the  project area.: Irrigation, drainage, ploughing, planting operations, and forest operations are identified in the PD as potential  project activities that could impact soil carbon in the project area.  These activities are evaluated and considered  unlikely to lead to a long-term increase in GHG emissions.   Irrigation activities are anticipated in the agroforestry and silvopasture lands, however no concrete evidence of  this was found by the audit team in the field visit. It is unlikely that the scale of irrigation activities that may be  implemented, would lead to increase in soil GHG emissions.    Regarding drainage activities, the audit team verified that no such activity took place in the project area.   In the management units visited, the audit team confirmed that ploughing was done according to the PD, which  considers  minimum  impacts  to  the  soil.  Conservation,  Agroforestry  and  Silvopastural  plots  are  predominantly  native species, and the high observed and projected survival rates indicates it is unlikely that ploughing will be  required in the future except on new planting areas. Commercial plots consist mostly of teak plantations which  do not require activities considered likely to significantly impact soil carbon. | :

Evidence must be given that the project contributes to a more sustainable development than the most  likely without-project scenario, short-, mid- and long-term.: The baseline scenario is clearly the continuation of the pre project activities, which in all the management unit  scenarios is the continuation of cattle on degraded lands predominantly covered by aggressive grass and related  degradation  of  unproductive  agricultural  land  to  pasture.  To  the  contrary,  the  implementation  of  the  project  pursues  conservation  and  commercial  purposes  generating  local  benefits  to  the  environment  and  project  participants from short to the long-term. T   a   b 1.  The baseline is the ‘woody biomass’ and ‘non-woody biomass’ on the eligible planting area just before the  planting start.  he calculation can be done in two different ways:  .   By executing field measurements. Here,  the  ‘Forest  Inventory’  guideline shall be applied.  .   By estimating the biomass in reference to similar areas  (cid:120)  regional and national default values shall preferably be used  (cid:120)  international default values can only be used if other values are not available: C   T   T The developer has resolved the issue the  CAR was based  on, having chosen a  different value for the baseline  carbon stocks, which is described below in the findings for Corrective Action Request 09-02.  Therefore the issue  of citing the dry matter value incorrectly as carbon, has been resolved.    CAR closed  orrective  Action  Request  09-02:  ACP  shall  include  remnant  trees  besides  the  tropical  moist  grassland,  to  determine baseline carbon stocks in the management units.  Feedback project developer (09 September 2013):  The developer has chosen a different method for estimating baseline carbon stocks which is more conservative  and does adequately address the concern that remnant and isolated trees were not accurately quantified in the  original baseline carbon stock estimate, which was based on grassland stocks.  The new carbon stock values for  the  baseline  are  different  for  commercial/conservation  management  units  and  for  agroforestry/silvopasture  management units.    he new value for commercial/conservation management units is 23tCO2/ha.  The new value for silvopasture and  agroforestry management units is 31tCO2/ha.  Conclusion of the certifier (27. September 2013):  The revised biomass carbon stock for commercial and conservation areas is derived from an academic paper based  on field measurements in Colombia.  This paper cites biomass stocks 8 tdm/ha.  This PDD reports the value as for  total  dry  biomass,  including  above  and  belowground  pools  (8  *1.58  to  estimate  belowground  biomass).   Converted to CO2 the carbon stocks is 23tCO2/ha.  The audit team has reviewed the PDF output of the carbon  calculations  on  the  Climate  Projects  registry  (File:  Management-Units  ACP  CFS.pdf)  and  confirmed  that  the  baseline  carbon  values  have  been  updated.    The  audit  team  has  additionally  reviewed  the  FOFI  model  and  confirmed that this has been updated as well.  The new carbon values are in conformance with the standard in  that they are calculated without some of the errors observed during the field audit (See CAR 09-01), are based on  scientific  literature,  and  are  calculated  correctly.    The  audit  team  observed  during  the  field  audit  that  the  commercial  and  conservation  areas  had  minimal  remnant  trees  from  before  project  establishment  and  were  mostly  saccharum  grass.    The  revised  baseline  stocks  are  acceptable  for  commercial  and  conservation  management units.     he revised biomass carbon stock for silvopasture and agroforestry systems is derived from an academic paper  also based on field measurements in Colombia of pasture systems with remnant trees.  This paper cites biomass  stocks as 17.2 tdm/ha.  This value is also for total dry biomass, including above and belowground pools.  Converted  to CO2 the value becomes 31tCO2/ha.   The audit team has reviewed the PDF output of the carbon calculations  on the Climate Projects registry (File: Management-Units ACP CFS.pdf) and confirmed that the baseline carbon  values have been updated.  The audit team has additionally reviewed the FOFI model and Baseline section of the  PD and confirmed that this has been updated as well.  The revised value of 31tCO2/ha for the silvopasture and  agroforestry management units is acceptable and in conformance with the standard in that it reflects the site  conditions  observed  during  the  field  audit  in  which  remnant  trees  from  before  the  project  start  date,  were  observed.    The  audit  team  noted  considerable  variability  between  different  sites  within  the  agroforestry  and  silvopasture management units, with some sites having no remnant trees and others having several.  This value  from analogous locations in a scientific paper is acceptable.

F indings from Field Audit: The vast majority of the project area was without protective vegetation in the baseline scenario, and the planting area was 100%  overrun by the introduced and invasive weed Saccharum spontaneum in the conservation and commercial reforestation areas.  The  soils  of  commercial  areas  were  also  covered  by  Saccharum  spontaneum.    The  agroforestry  areas  did  not  meet  the  definition of forest, but had lower presence of Saccharum spontaneum nonetheless. Ciri Grande area has soils of low productive  capacity. The use of land was not in sync with the land’s natural ability, and was generating negative impacts on the productive  base  and negative externalities such  as erosion and loss of  biodiversity.    The  updated  Sustainability  Monitoring  Plan  outlines  how biodiversity and socioeconomic improvement will be monitored, and these are discussed at greater length in the project’s  various management plans.   The project demonstrates conformance.

(j)  Land-use history and current situation of the project area: R The historical use was the traditional agriculture of slash and burn and an extensive cattle ranch. The area of  Cirí and Trinidad has soils of low productive capacity. Land use was not in line with natural capacity, it was  generating negative impacts on the productive base and negative externalities such as erosion and loss of  biodiversity. Consideration on protection, type of soil, flora, fauna and soil is detailed in the PDD, at present  similar uses are maintained in the surrounding areas. It is important to mention again that the project is  dispersed in different areas of the Canal Watershed. The conservation and commercial projects were  established in areas invaded by the weeds known as white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  eforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity of the areas. Native species are used in a mixture  that seeks to reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the  gradual recovery of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the  improvement of surface runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  In agroforestry and silvopastoral activities, natural resource management is promoted, integrating  communities as a fundamental part of their development, with a focus on property planning, sustainability  and protection of natural resources, providing an economic productive option for communities by

D   I   I further characterizes the soil types present in the project area.  The management plans for Ciri Grande and Rio Trinidad specify  establishment of tree cover as being the principal mitigating measure against soil erosion, complemented by the use of organic  inputs  or  limited  use  of  chemical  inputs  and  eliminating  slash  and  burn  monoculture  practices.    These  efforts  combined  are  expected to improve biotic communities and reduce soil compaction.    The audit team agrees.  uring the July 2016 field audit, the audit team visited several commercial planting area modelling units.  These planting areas  had a network of dirt roads for the transportation of personnel and materials.  During the rainy season these roads apparently  can become channels for runoff, producing eroded soils and possibly leading to stream sedimentation.  The roads did not have  any improvement made to them to mitigate erosion, such as water bars, drainage ditches, or other such improvements.    n response to the 2016 Performance Certification audit the ACP presented a proposal for mitigation measures protecting soil in  internal  roads  within  the  project’s  commercial  plantations.    The  proposal’s  action  items  are  the  development  and  phased  implementation of mitigation measures against the negative soil impacts from the internal roads.  The proposal also contains  a  timeline  of  when  the  different  project  activities  would  begin,  starting  in  January  2017.    This  is  significant  progress  towards  a  meaningful future solution, thus FAR 01/16 has been opened.  tem 26) of the Do-No-Harm Assessment has been deemed irrelevant; the audit team concurs.: NCR/OBS | : FAR 01/16  The internal road network for the commercial planting areas were observed to be subject to  erosion and there were no mitigation measures in place to protect the soils.  The project has drafted an  action and mitigation plan, the effectiveness of which shall be evaluated during the following Performance  Certification audit.

Evidence must be given that project activities will NOT lead to a long-term increase of greenhouse gas  emissions in the  carbon  pool  ‘soil’  on  the  project area.: Irrigation, drainage, ploughing, planting operations, and forest operations are identified in the PD as potential  project activities that could impact soil carbon in the project area.  These activities are evaluated and considered  unlikely to lead to a long-term increase in GHG emissions.   Irrigation activities are anticipated in the agroforestry and silvopasture lands, however no concrete evidence of  this was found by the audit team in the field visit. It is unlikely that the scale of irrigation activities that may be  implemented, would lead to increase in soil GHG emissions.    Regarding drainage activities, the audit team verified that no such activity took place in the project area.   In the management units visited, the audit team confirmed that ploughing was done according to the PD, which  considers  minimum  impacts  to  the  soil.  Conservation,  Agroforestry  and  Silvopastural  plots  are  predominantly  native species, and the high observed and projected survival rates indicates it is unlikely that ploughing will be  required in the future except on new planting areas. Commercial plots consist mostly of teak plantations which  do not require activities considered likely to significantly impact soil carbon. | :

F indings from Field Audit: The vast majority of the project area was without protective vegetation in the baseline scenario, and the planting area was 100%  overrun by the introduced and invasive weed Saccharum spontaneum in the conservation and commercial reforestation areas.  The  soils  of  commercial  areas  were  also  covered  by  Saccharum  spontaneum.    The  agroforestry  areas  did  not  meet  the  definition of forest, but had lower presence of Saccharum spontaneum nonetheless. Ciri Grande area has soils of low productive  capacity. The use of land was not in sync with the land’s natural ability, and was generating negative impacts on the productive  base  and negative externalities such  as erosion and loss of  biodiversity.    The  updated  Sustainability  Monitoring  Plan  outlines  how biodiversity and socioeconomic improvement will be monitored, and these are discussed at greater length in the project’s  various management plans.   The project demonstrates conformance.

D   I   I further characterizes the soil types present in the project area.  The management plans for Ciri Grande and Rio Trinidad specify  establishment of tree cover as being the principal mitigating measure against soil erosion, complemented by the use of organic  inputs  or  limited  use  of  chemical  inputs  and  eliminating  slash  and  burn  monoculture  practices.    These  efforts  combined  are  expected to improve biotic communities and reduce soil compaction.    The audit team agrees.  uring the July 2016 field audit, the audit team visited several commercial planting area modelling units.  These planting areas  had a network of dirt roads for the transportation of personnel and materials.  During the rainy season these roads apparently  can become channels for runoff, producing eroded soils and possibly leading to stream sedimentation.  The roads did not have  any improvement made to them to mitigate erosion, such as water bars, drainage ditches, or other such improvements.    n response to the 2016 Performance Certification audit the ACP presented a proposal for mitigation measures protecting soil in  internal  roads  within  the  project’s  commercial  plantations.    The  proposal’s  action  items  are  the  development  and  phased  implementation of mitigation measures against the negative soil impacts from the internal roads.  The proposal also contains  a  timeline  of  when  the  different  project  activities  would  begin,  starting  in  January  2017.    This  is  significant  progress  towards  a  meaningful future solution, thus FAR 01/16 has been opened.  tem 26) of the Do-No-Harm Assessment has been deemed irrelevant; the audit team concurs.: Conformance | : Yes     No     N/A

What is the geographic scope of the proj.content: Findings from Field Audit: The auditors were provided with a copy of the project’s Key Project Information Documentation that covered elements a) – p) as  required by GS; the information provided was generally considered accurate by the audit team based on review of the project  documentation and on-site observation during the 2016 Performance Certification audit.     a)  Project  activities  –  The  project  proponent  has  designed  a  carbon  project  involving  assisted  natural  regeneration  (the  conservation  planting  areas)  and  reforestation  (the  commercial  and  agroforestry  planting  areas).    The  commercial  planting  areas  are  on  government-owned  land,  whereas  the  agroforestry  planting  areas  are  found  on  local  farmers’  lands, and the conservation areas are a mix of both land ownership types.  b)  Project participants – The local consultative councils are key representatives of the local landowners and play an active  role  in  stakeholder  participation  within  the  context  of  the  project.    Other  groups  include  the  local  Coffee  Producers’  Association  (ACACPA),  the  Ministry  of  the  Environment,  Ministry  of  Agricultural  Development,  the  GIZ,  Fundación  Natura,  CAF  and  FOMIN,  the  last  three  of  whom  have  been  involved  in  supporting  the  coffee  producers  in  implementing sustainable natural resource management practices.  c)  Communities involved in the project – The communities involved in the project are:  Santa Rosa, Trinidad, Cirí Grande,  Cacao (Alto de las Minas, Arenosa, Bajo Bonito, Bella  vista, Bonga Abajo, Bonga Centro, Chileno, Cirí de los Sotos,  Cirí Grande, Ciricito, Ciricito Abajo, Ciricito Arriba, Clara Abajo, El Harino, El Limón, Faldares, Gasparillal, La Bonga, La  Florida,  las  gaitas,  Las  Minas,  las  Negritas,  Limón  Raudal,  Paraíso,  Peñas  Blancas,  Santa  Fe,  Sigual,  Teria,  Teria  Nacimiento, Vista Alegre.  There are additional communities nearby the commercial and conservation planting areas in  Chilibre,  San  Vicente,  Parque  Nacional  Soberanía,  Chagres  and  Burunga,  but these communities are  not  involved  in  the project.  d)  Location of the project area and the planting area – The entirety of the project area is located within the Panama Canal  Watershed.    The  sub-watersheds  are:  Cirí  y  Trinidad,  Chilibre,  San  Vicente,  Caribe,  Potabilizadora,  Parque  Nacional  Soberanía, Parque Nacional Camino de Cruces, Chagres y Burunga.  e)  Size of the project area and the planting area – The project area and planting area are 1,940 hectares.

W f)   Risk of the project area to change during the crediting period – The project proponent contracts companies for 5 years  at a time in order to ensure proper management and maintenance of the project areas.  Further, the agreements signed  with local landowners stipulate the same.    g)  Risk  of  the  project  activities  to  change  during  the  crediting  period  –  The  project  activities  are  continually  being  implemented and the project intends to expand the project and planting areas for future certification.  h)  Timeframe for the project activities – The project will last 30 years.  i)   Number of predicted CO2 certificates – The project estimates that there are 132,246 tCO2e of verifiable CO2 certificates  at the time of the 2016 Performance Certification.  j)   Land-use history and current situation of the project area – Slash and burn agriculture, followed by cattle grazing, has  dominated the landscape.  These land use practices continue outside of the project intervention.  k)  Socio-economic history and current situation – The socio-economic context of the project area is comprised of poor rural  subsistence farmers.  l)   Forest management applied (past and future) – Past forest management has been a process of continual degradation  from forested to agricultural and pasture lands.  Current forest management under the project is fomenting sustainable  land and forest management with the intent of increasing environmental services and reducing CO2 emissions.  m)   Forest characteristics – Neither the pre-project land use nor the baseline scenario involve forest management, as the  pre-existing  forests  are  no  more.    The  project  describes  the  incorporation  of  various  tree  species  in  to  the  project  landscape,  principally  depending  on  native  species  in  the  agroforestry  and  conservation  planting  areas,  and  teak/eucalyptus in the commercial areas.  n)  Main social impacts – The project counts on an improvement of environmental services for the surrounding communities  and  the  whole  of  the  Panama  Canal  Zone  and  an  increase  in  coffee-driven  economic  improvement  to  be  the  main  social impacts of the project.  The audit team agrees.  o)  Main environmental impacts – The project expects the project’s implementation will lead to an increase in forest cover  will  facilitate  a  biodiversity-rebound,  advanced  carbon  sequestration  and  storage,  and  overall  improvement  of  water  quality and erosion reduction.  p) Financial structure – The project is funded primarily by the Panamanian government, supplemented by proceeds earned  from the sale of CO2 certificates.    here there have been no changes to information in the various elements of the Key Project Information documentation, such  an indication would help clarify that the existing information still remains relevant. (j)  Land-use history and current situation of the project area: R The historical use was the traditional agriculture of slash and burn and an extensive cattle ranch. The area of  Cirí and Trinidad has soils of low productive capacity. Land use was not in line with natural capacity, it was  generating negative impacts on the productive base and negative externalities such as erosion and loss of  biodiversity. Consideration on protection, type of soil, flora, fauna and soil is detailed in the PDD, at present  similar uses are maintained in the surrounding areas. It is important to mention again that the project is  dispersed in different areas of the Canal Watershed. The conservation and commercial projects were  established in areas invaded by the weeds known as white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  eforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity of the areas. Native species are used in a mixture  that seeks to reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the  gradual recovery of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the  improvement of surface runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  In agroforestry and silvopastoral activities, natural resource management is promoted, integrating  communities as a fundamental part of their development, with a focus on property planning, sustainability  and protection of natural resources, providing an economic productive option for communities by

The project has drafted an  action and mitigation plan, the effectiveness of which shall be evaluated during the following Performance  Certification audit.

D   I   I further characterizes the soil types present in the project area.  The management plans for Ciri Grande and Rio Trinidad specify  establishment of tree cover as being the principal mitigating measure against soil erosion, complemented by the use of organic  inputs  or  limited  use  of  chemical  inputs  and  eliminating  slash  and  burn  monoculture  practices.    These  efforts  combined  are  expected to improve biotic communities and reduce soil compaction.    The audit team agrees.  uring the July 2016 field audit, the audit team visited several commercial planting area modelling units.  These planting areas  had a network of dirt roads for the transportation of personnel and materials.  During the rainy season these roads apparently  can become channels for runoff, producing eroded soils and possibly leading to stream sedimentation.  The roads did not have  any improvement made to them to mitigate erosion, such as water bars, drainage ditches, or other such improvements.    n response to the 2016 Performance Certification audit the ACP presented a proposal for mitigation measures protecting soil in  internal  roads  within  the  project’s  commercial  plantations.    The  proposal’s  action  items  are  the  development  and  phased  implementation of mitigation measures against the negative soil impacts from the internal roads.  The proposal also contains  a  timeline  of  when  the  different  project  activities  would  begin,  starting  in  January  2017. indings from Field Audit: 1   T   I   2 The  project  achieved  a Gold  Level  CCBA  validation  on  March  30,  2012.  Following  the  combined  certification  procedures,  the  CFS recognized the certification as replacement of the criteria contained in ‘Land & CO2 Tenure” chapter requirements for the  project’s initial certification.  As this is a performance certification for the project areas that were already initially certified under  CFS,  there  are  no  updates for  the  PP  to  provide  in  the  GS  templates.    Nevertheless,  the  project  proponent  has  provided  the  audit team with the “Project Participants & Secured Titles” Gold Standard template, which we evaluate here:  :  (a)  The PP identifies itself as the Legal Project Owner and provides its name and mailing address.  ACP functionary Angel  Ureña is identified in the template as the primary contact for the project.  (b)  The  PP  contends  that  the  Panama  Canal  Authority  does  not  have  a  legal  registration  number,  and  that  the  transition  from CFS to GS was successfully completed in 2014, at which time the appropriate documentation was submitted.  he project has included discussion on Items A-E as required.  The project has also submitted an example usufruct beneficiary  agreement and a bidding form.    n  sum,  the  landowner  expressly  agrees  to  project  intervention  in  the  form  of  reforestation,  agroforestry,  or  Silvopastoral  modalities.  Both the ACP and the owner agree expressly that the exclusive rights of environmental services, referred to in this  contract,  exclusive  limited  and  specifically  the  ability  to  capture  carbon  generated  from  the  activity  or  activities  indicated.   Therefore, GHG reductions resulting from this contract may be marketed by the ACP.  The ACP will be responsible for the initial  investments related to the establishment and maintenance of the planting areas.  The ACP provides financial resources and hire  companies to develop the work.  All workers in the agroforestry modelling units are given an incentive for daily wage labor.  :  (a)  Gathering  information  from  the  Project  Participants  &  Secured  Titles  template  and  the  project’s  CFS  PDD,  the  initial  certification report, and additional documentation provided by the PP, the PP has entered in to a usufructuary agreement  with the landowners, granting the ACP rightful use over the carbon sequestration rights in the project area.  As evidence  that the contracts have been signed by both landowners and the ACP the PP submitted a letter to the companies it was  consulting  with for  technical  expertise  on  the  project—Forest  Finance  and  CO2OL  Carbon  Consulting—stating  that  all  the  beneficiaries  have  signed  the  contract  (Document  #33).    The  project  has  submitted  a  sample  of  these  signed  contracts to demonstrate that 100% of the project beneficiaries have signed the agreement.

W f)   Risk of the project area to change during the crediting period – The project proponent contracts companies for 5 years  at a time in order to ensure proper management and maintenance of the project areas.  Further, the agreements signed  with local landowners stipulate the same.    g)  Risk  of  the  project  activities  to  change  during  the  crediting  period  –  The  project  activities  are  continually  being  implemented and the project intends to expand the project and planting areas for future certification.  h)  Timeframe for the project activities – The project will last 30 years.  i)   Number of predicted CO2 certificates – The project estimates that there are 132,246 tCO2e of verifiable CO2 certificates  at the time of the 2016 Performance Certification.  j)   Land-use history and current situation of the project area – Slash and burn agriculture, followed by cattle grazing, has  dominated the landscape.  These land use practices continue outside of the project intervention.  k)  Socio-economic history and current situation – The socio-economic context of the project area is comprised of poor rural  subsistence farmers.  l)   Forest management applied (past and future) – Past forest management has been a process of continual degradation  from forested to agricultural and pasture lands.  Current forest management under the project is fomenting sustainable  land and forest management with the intent of increasing environmental services and reducing CO2 emissions.  m)   Forest characteristics – Neither the pre-project land use nor the baseline scenario involve forest management, as the  pre-existing  forests  are  no  more.    The  project  describes  the  incorporation  of  various  tree  species  in  to  the  project  landscape,  principally  depending  on  native  species  in  the  agroforestry  and  conservation  planting  areas,  and  teak/eucalyptus in the commercial areas.  n)  Main social impacts – The project counts on an improvement of environmental services for the surrounding communities  and  the  whole  of  the  Panama  Canal  Zone  and  an  increase  in  coffee-driven  economic  improvement  to  be  the  main  social impacts of the project.  The audit team agrees.  o)  Main environmental impacts – The project expects the project’s implementation will lead to an increase in forest cover  will  facilitate  a  biodiversity-rebound,  advanced  carbon  sequestration  and  storage,  and  overall  improvement  of  water  quality and erosion reduction.  p) Financial structure – The project is funded primarily by the Panamanian government, supplemented by proceeds earned  from the sale of CO2 certificates.    here there have been no changes to information in the various elements of the Key Project Information documentation, such  an indication would help clarify that the existing information still remains relevant. The  project  describes  the  incorporation  of  various  tree  species  in  to  the  project  landscape,  principally  depending  on  native  species  in  the  agroforestry  and  conservation  planting  areas,  and  teak/eucalyptus in the commercial areas.  n)  Main social impacts – The project counts on an improvement of environmental services for the surrounding communities  and  the  whole  of  the  Panama  Canal  Zone  and  an  increase  in  coffee-driven  economic  improvement  to  be  the  main  social impacts of the project.  The audit team agrees.  o)  Main environmental impacts – The project expects the project’s implementation will lead to an increase in forest cover  will  facilitate  a  biodiversity-rebound,  advanced  carbon  sequestration  and  storage,  and  overall  improvement  of  water  quality and erosion reduction.  p) Financial structure – The project is funded primarily by the Panamanian government, supplemented by proceeds earned  from the sale of CO2 certificates.    here there have been no changes to information in the various elements of the Key Project Information documentation, such  an indication would help clarify that the existing information still remains relevant. L   H   N 1.  Evidence must be given that the project is not business as usual. Therefore, the additionality analysis  must be executed according to the latest version A/R CDM ‘Combined tool to identify the baseline  scenario and demonstrate additionality in A/R CDM project activities’.  ink:  http://cdm.unfccc.int/methodologies/ARmethodologies/tools/  ereby, the first Point of STEP 0 can be omitted.  ote that some terms in this tool (e.g. CERs, A/R CDM project) differ from the terms defined by the  CFS and shall be interpreted accordingly.: S   T   S Pre-project land use:  There are effectively no  barriers to this scenario.  S. spontaneum is a globally renowned  invasive  grass  species  and  there  are  well  documented  challenges  to  its  restoration  in  many  areas  including  Panama.  It is very unlikely that without human intervention, that these areas would be reforested naturally given  the likelihood of fire and the state of degradation.  Reforestation activities in the absence of carbon finance:  There are reforestation projects in Panama but it is well  documented that they do tend to happen in areas with better soil that are less dominated by Saccharum.  The  project  had  some  clear  MUs  that  failed  to  be  reforested  because  the  implementing  people  did  not  have  the  technical  ability  to  successfully  manage  the  invasive  grass.  There  are  clear  investment  and  technical  barriers.   Interviews with local farmer confirmed in the agroforestry and silvopasture areas that the farmers did not have  the capacity to reforest the land, or the technical knowledge.    Cattle ranching:  There are technological barriers to silvopasture system implementation in cattle raising areas.   This was confirmed through interviews with farmers during the field audit.  tep 3:  The project confronts many of the typical challenges of smallholder based PES programs.  Project costs are high  as they require training and coordination of hundreds of smallholder members as well as experimental eradication  of  an  invasive  grass.    Project  benefits  are  primarily  ecosystem  services  which  are  non  monetized  other  than  through carbon finance.  The project will earn some money from timber harvesting in the Commercial plantation  areas (other areas will not generate revenue for the project).    he additionality assessment compares the revenue earned only from the commercial plantation (other planting  areas do not generate revenue) to the IRR of a Panamanian government bond and the interest rate of a saving  account in Panama.  Appropriate references are provided for these alternatives.  Without the carbon revenue the  ACP project is less financially attractive (IRR is lower) than the two alternative options.  With the assumption of  sales of carbon credits, the project becomes either more attractive than the government bond, or more attractive  than both the government  bond and the savings account, depending on the hypothetical carbon credit  price.   Carbon credit prices (Table 02-01) are credible given credit prices for CarbonFix projects cited in recent reports.   For  example  the  2013  State  of  the  Voluntary  Carbon  Market  report  (Ecosystem  Marketplace)  cited  a  2011  CarbonFix average offset price of $10.7/tCO2e.  It is clear from the analysis that the project activity without carbon  credits is less profitable than alternative options because i) the IRR is less than the expected IRR of a Panamanian  Savings Account or a Government Bond, and ii) the IRR only becomes higher than these in the assumption of well  priced carbon credits.  Further, this assessment only evaluates the revenue earned from the Commercial planting  area.  The agroforestry, silvopasture, and conservation Management Units have substantial up front costs and are  in effect subsidized by the future revenue that may be earned from the commercial area.  There will be no revenue  earned from these other three project activities.    tep 4:   The proponent  appropriately lists similar project  activities by different  actors in the country with and without  carbon  finance.    There  are  other  similar  plantation  projects  in  the  country  that  do  not  use  carbon  finance,  however, these project are either 80% or 100% Teak (Tectona grandis) plantations and none of them include the  substantial  Conservation  Management  Units,  Silvopatorial  Management  Units,  and  Agroforestry  Management  Units that are a core part of the ACP project.  The Commercial Management Units are the only part of ACP that is  comparable.    Additionally,  there  are  projects  developed  by  Forest  Finance  that  are  similar  to  the  commercial  Management Units of ACP, but these areas are also certified to CCB, a carbon standard.

(k)  Socio-economic history and current situation: The socio-economic situation of the area is low-income subsistence and livestock farming. As organizations  involved in the project are: the Association of Coffee Producers of the Cirí and Trinidad Rivers of the Panama  Canal, whose acronym is ACACPA, formed by the ACP, to work in a group way for the marketing and selling  of coffee . The local committees and advisory councils of Ciri and Trinidad, also made up of the ACP; the  Ministry of Environment (MiAmbiente) and the Ministry of Agriculture(MIDA). As Technical Cooperation, GIZ  (German Cooperation) has supported the development of the carbon dioxide estimation methodology  established by the plantations and the forest inventory of the certified plantations. Also, the support of the  NATURA Foundation, CAF and FOMIN, is strengthened in the association of coffee growers of the area. See  PDD.

By estimating the biomass in reference to similar areas  (cid:120)  regional and national default values shall preferably be used  (cid:120)  international default values can only be used if other values are not available: 1.  See NCR (09-01):    The  default  value  (6.2)  is  measured  in  “tonnes  d.m.  ha-1”  (tonnes  of  dry  matter  per   hectare), however, the model applies the 6.2t d.m. ha as if it were 6.2tC per ha.  This is evident in review  of the Baseline section of the PDD, as well as through discussion with members of the developer team.   This error actually reduces the net CO2 Fixation of the project.  Based on conversations during the audit,  the developer will correctly apply the value for the sake of accuracy.  2.  The audit team has observed that, although much of the planting areas are dominated by Saccharum  spontaneum, there are also significant remnant trees and palms.  This varies by Management Unit, and  Polygon.    Review  of  the  GIS  shapefiles,  as  well  as  observations  in  the  field  has  confirmed  that  the  remaining  trees  and  patches  of  trees  were  often  not  removed  from  the  planting  area.    As  such,  the  baseline  is  actually  tropical  moist  grassland  with  remnant  trees.    Failure  to  incorporate  this  into  the   baseline carbon stocks will result in an overestimation of the project benefits in future monitoring events  when  the  remnant  trees  that  were  not  planted  by  the  project  will  likely  be  included  in  the  forest  inventory

(j)  Land-use history and current situation of the project area: R The historical use was the traditional agriculture of slash and burn and an extensive cattle ranch. The area of  Cirí and Trinidad has soils of low productive capacity. Land use was not in line with natural capacity, it was  generating negative impacts on the productive base and negative externalities such as erosion and loss of  biodiversity. Consideration on protection, type of soil, flora, fauna and soil is detailed in the PDD, at present  similar uses are maintained in the surrounding areas. It is important to mention again that the project is  dispersed in different areas of the Canal Watershed. The conservation and commercial projects were  established in areas invaded by the weeds known as white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  eforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity of the areas. Native species are used in a mixture  that seeks to reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the  gradual recovery of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the  improvement of surface runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  In agroforestry and silvopastoral activities, natural resource management is promoted, integrating  communities as a fundamental part of their development, with a focus on property planning, sustainability  and protection of natural resources, providing an economic productive option for communities by

Evidence must be given that at least 10% of the project area is managed  a.  as a nature conservation area  OR  b.  to meet a national or sub-national HCV area definition.   riterion 4. does not have to be fulfilled in case more than 30% of the project area is managed  according to chapter ’06  CO2-fixation  -  Option  1b)  Conservation  forest’.: a.  At least 10% of the total project area will be managed as conservation area. From the project are equivalent to 10 000 hectares, 1770 (ha) were designated for  Conservation purposes. From the 2985 hectares selected as eligible planting area, 910 hectares were selected for conservation purposes (MU 06-14).   The ACP reforestation project intention considers the importance of forest close to the watersheds. Forests can easily absorbed water acting like sponges and  preventing from possible flooding. In dry seasons through percolation water can be drained back to the river if more water is necessary. These areas are also of  great importance for preventing soil erosion and possible landslides.

1.  A description of the project’s general forest management objectives must be given.:  | : For all the 4 activities of the project land preparation will be a mix:  Manually or mechanically, depending on the topography of the place. To  prevent soil erosion. The land used to implement the reforestation project was before covered with  Saccharum spontaneum or degraded  soil that was used for catting. In both cases it is necessary ploughing to give the soil the right texture before planting. In the case of  catting  areas the soil is compacted and there is a higher impermeability. Ploughing is employed in flat areas and avoided in areas with slopes. This  is a security principle to avoid post erosion from ploughing. No slash and burn technics are used for land preparation.  ( Ref.03-02, page 1, points a,b,)(Ref.03-04, page 1, point a,b,c)

3.  Planting area is NOT eligible, if the land:  a.  was deforested and thereafter replanted in order to generate CO2-certificates OR  b.  is wetland OR  c.  is situated on ground that is permafrost OR  d.  is agriculture farming land and threatens the food security of the local population through the  conversion to forest.: a.  There  is  no  evidence  in  the  documentation  or  from  the  field  audit  to  indicate  that  the  planting  area  was  deforested and later replanted to generate CO2 certificates.  Deforestation, as verified through the remote  sensing analysis and through stakeholder interviews typically occurred in the 1960-1980s.  Additionally, most  all Management Units contain remnant trees from before the planting began.  b.  In the Eligibility section of the PD, the proponent does not provide sufficient demonstration that the planting  areas do not contain wetlands.  The proponent only provides a map of the RAMSAR recognized wetlands of  Panama.  RAMSAR wetlands are those officially recognized as having global importance (FAR 01-01).  c.  The project is in the tropics and clearly permafrost is not an issue.  The proponent documents this.  d.  The  Conservation  and  Commercial  Management  Units  occur  on  areas  predominantly  covered  with  Paja  canalera  (Saccharum  spontaneum),  an  invasive  grass  which  is  very  hard  to  remove.    Hence  no  agriculture  occurs on these lands.  The agroforestry and silvopasture systems occur on either low productivity agricultural  land  and/or  cattle  pasture.    The  proponent  cites  scientific  literature  sources  providing  support  that  these  activities will increase the productivity of the areas, possibly enhancing food security. | :

5.  Evidence must be given that project activities will NOT lead to a long-term increase of greenhouse gas  emissions in the  carbon  pool  ‘soil’  on  the  project area.: Irrigation, drainage, ploughing, planting operations, and forest operations are identified in the PD as potential  project activities that could impact soil carbon in the project area.  These activities are evaluated and considered  unlikely to lead to a long-term increase in GHG emissions.   Irrigation activities are anticipated in the agroforestry and silvopasture lands, however no concrete evidence of  this was found by the audit team in the field visit. It is unlikely that the scale of irrigation activities that may be  implemented, would lead to increase in soil GHG emissions.    Regarding drainage activities, the audit team verified that no such activity took place in the project area.   In the management units visited, the audit team confirmed that ploughing was done according to the PD, which  considers  minimum  impacts  to  the  soil.  Conservation,  Agroforestry  and  Silvopastural  plots  are  predominantly  native species, and the high observed and projected survival rates indicates it is unlikely that ploughing will be  required in the future except on new planting areas. Commercial plots consist mostly of teak plantations which  do not require activities considered likely to significantly impact soil carbon. | :

The evidence from PDD_ACP_CFS.pdf, Page 15, mentions the project's focus on sustainable land use practices, which is also not directly related to the adoption of alternative measures by farmers. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

C AR#:: The project does not adequately justify the scores given for risk elements ‘water’, ‘wind’, ‘animals’, ‘fire’, ‘diseases’, or ‘irregular  resettlement or illicit crop production’.  For these same elements, the project does not describe the measures needed and taken  to  ensure  the  survival  and  permanence  of  carbon  stocks  (in  the  plantations,  agroforestry  areas  AND  conservation  Modelling  Units) in planting areas that it will establish when the project eventually expands as intended.  For the water risk element, the  project does not present any information related to the water requirements of the projects’ species composition.  Furthermore,  the seasonal monsoons may present a threat to the carbon permanence, as witnessed by a heavy storm the week of the 2016  field audit.  In the PDD the project identifies several potential pests and diseases (“Corticium salmonicolor, Hypsipyla grandela,  defoliation  by  leafcutter  ants,  wood  infection  by  termites  and  followed  by  fungi”)  that  could  affect  some  of  the  thirty  species  utilized by the project.  The PP does not discuss the incidence of these pests and how the project is effectively mitigating against  the risk to permanence that they pose. | 35/16:

1.  Leakage is caused by an increase of emissions outside of the project area as a result of    the project activity.  Leakage emissions can be caused due to a shift of the following activities:     a.   d.    agricultural farming  fuelwood use         b.   e.    resettlement  charcoal burning         c.   f.     livestock grazing  timber harvesting: Given that the project aims to integrate the forest component into other socioeconomic activities (agriculture and  grassland through agroforestry and silvopasture systems) rather than impose the strict protection of the existing  forest, the PDD anticipated no leakage for agroforestry/silvopasture MUs, where the majority of the population  lives.  The audit team evaluated this assertion in the field via observation and interviews with participating farmers  and  concluded  it  was  a  reasonable  assertion.    The  existing  agricultural  and  pasture  systems  are  very  low  productivity, mostly due to the dominance of Saccharum spontaneum. It is reasonable to assume equal or higher  yields under agroforestry and silvopasture systems.    It is assumed that the forest within the project area will supply materials for domestic use (fire wood/timber for  familiar consumption), with an extension service to ensure sustainable production. In this way, there is no reason  to seek material outside the project area. Therefore, no displacement is foreseen in agroforestry and silvopasture  plots.   The audit team also visited the commercial plots and found no evidence of displacement of agriculture, cattle or  other activities from the project area to the surrounding areas. | :

Planting in the remaining  area  is  done  mechanically,  using  agricultural   tractors  equipped  with  disks.  The manual preparation of the land is preferred due to its low impact on soil. See OBS 04/16  he project argues that the fertilizer element of the ‘Other Emissions’ template is irrelevant as the plantations were established  prior to the Initial Certification under CFS in 2011.    he project proponent has provided the following justification in the Do-No-Harm Assessment template:  Soil  tests  in  commercial  plantation  areas  revealed  acidic  soils  with  low  phosphorus  content,  so  fertilizer  application  is  recommended.    It  was  recommended  in  commercial  area  mixing  chemical  fertilizers  with  organic  matter  at  the  bottom  of  the  planting hole and buried.  he  Trinidad  area  has  soils  of  low  productive  capacity.  The  use  of  land  not  in  keeping  with  the  natural  ability,  is  generating  negative impacts on the productive base and negative externalities such as erosion and loss of biodiversity. In consideration of  soil protection, soil type, flora, fauna and soil, it is advisable to use fertilizers to improve productivity.  he terms of reference indicate the mix of fertilizer with organic fertilizer in the bottom of the hole at planting and buried during  maintenance in the early years, to increase their effectiveness and reduce leaching or volatilization loss.”    he project provided additional supporting documentation containing recommendations for various organic and chemical inputs  for soil improvement.  The documents describe fertilizer types to be used, the appropriate application methods, and a proposed  frequency of application.    lso, the ACP presented a proposal for documentation of fertilizer use.  The proposal’s action items are the development and  phased implementation of data collection on fertilizer use.  The proposal also contains a timeline of when the different project  activities would begin, starting in January 2017.  This is significant progress towards a meaningful solution, and the CAR may  therefore be closed.  A FAR (02/16) has been opened.  Further, the project has made a conservative estimate of reductions in  the calculated CO2 certificates as a result of these emissions, amounting to 1,397 tCO2e during the period 2014-2016.

5. Gender equality.summary: The provided context does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures the proportion of women in managerial positions. The context repeatedly mentions the total number of employees (over 10,000) but does not specify the number of managerial positions or employees in such roles. The only mention of women is in the context of a nursery run by the women of the project, but this does not provide any information about their roles or positions within the organization. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Findings
Given that the project aims to integrate the forest component into other socioeconomic activities (agriculture and 
grassland through agroforestry and silvopasture systems) rather than impose the strict protection of the existing 
forest, the PDD anticipated no leakage for agroforestry/silvopasture MUs, where the majority of the population 
lives.

12. Responsible consumption and producti.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the average reduction in synthetic fertilizer consumption per ha. The context mentions the use of organic fertilizer and a total of 2148 tCO2 was deducted from the carbon calculation benefits of the project (Document: GS+Certification+Report+16.12.2013.pdf, Page: 170), but this does not provide information on the reduction in synthetic fertilizer consumption. The context also mentions that the project calculated a total of 1,397 tCO2e emissions from nitrogen fertilizer across the entire 1940 ha project areas (Document: GS+Performance+Certification+Report+09.29.2016.pdf, Page: 28), but this is related to organic fertilizer use, not synthetic fertilizer consumption. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only, and the score is 1.

By estimating the biomass in reference to similar areas  (cid:120)  regional and national default values shall preferably be used  (cid:120)  international default values can only be used if other values are not available: The developer chooses to use an international default value from the IPCC for Tropical Moist and Wet grassland  on  areas  after  conversion  from  another  land  use  (http://www.ipcc- nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2006gl/pdf/4_Volume4/V4_06_Ch6_Grassland.pdf).  The  developer  chooses  this  value  because of the prevalence of Paja canalera (S. spontaneum) in the project area and the fact that project activities  are deliberately located in these areas based on the original remote sensing analysis used.  However, based on  auditor observation in the field as well as review of the source document, there are two significant problems with  this default value.   The  default  value  (6.2)  is  measured  in  “tonnes  d.m.  ha-1”  (tonnes  of  dry  matter  per  hectare),  however,  the  model   applies the 6.2t d.m. ha as if it were 6.2tC per ha.  This is evident in review of the Baseline section of the PDD, as  well as through discussion with members of the developer team.  This error actually reduces the net CO2 Fixation  of the project.  Based on conversations during the audit, the developer will correctly apply the value for the sake  of accuracy. (NCR 09-01).  The audit team has observed that, although much of the planting areas are dominated by S. spontaneum, there  are also significant remnant trees and palms.  This varies by Management Unit, and Polygon.  Review of the GIS  shapefiles, as well as observations in the field has confirmed that the remaining trees and patches of trees were  often not removed from the planting area.  As such, the baseline is actually tropical moist grassland with remnant  trees.  Failure to incorporate this into the baseline carbon stocks will result in an overestimation of the project  benefits in future monitoring events when the remnant trees that were not planted by the project will likely be  included in the forest inventory. As  the  project does not have a registry documenting the use of nitrogen fertilizers, the project made a  conservative  estimate  of  the  amount  of  fertilizer  applied  since  the  last  certification  event  and  applied the appropriate corresponding emissions to the CO2 certificate calculations, which it will  continue to do until the fertilizer use registry is effectively implemented.  Using a factor of .005  tCO2e  of  emissions  for  each  kilogram  of  nitrogen  in  the  total  estimated  amount  of  fertilizer  used, the project proponent calculates a total of 1,397 tCO2e emissions from nitrogen fertilizer  across the entire 1940 ha project areas.  The project has updated the carbon calculations with  the new figure.  he CAR is closed.

(o)  Main environmental impacts (risks and benefits): T The projects developed increase the vegetal cover that improves the connectivity and the biodiversity.  Reforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity. Native species are used in a mixture that seeks to  reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the gradual recovery  of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the improvement of surface  runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).   he ACP presents actions for the protection and conservation of the water resources of the Watershed in  harmony with the activities developed by the inhabitants of the Basin. ACP and producers integrate  environmental, social and economic activities, generating profits for the company, for the country and for  the inhabitants of the Watershed. It is a commitment of corporate responsibility by the ACP and a  commitment of protection of the natural resources by the residents, while improving their living conditions.

(o)  Main environmental impacts (risks and benefits): T The projects developed increase the vegetal cover that improves the connectivity and the biodiversity.  Reforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity. Native species are used in a mixture that seeks to  reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the gradual recovery  of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the improvement of surface  runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).   he ACP presents actions for the protection and conservation of the water resources of the Watershed in  harmony with the activities developed by the inhabitants of the Basin. ACP and producers integrate  environmental, social and economic activities, generating profits for the company, for the country and for  the inhabitants of the Watershed. It is a commitment of corporate responsibility by the ACP and a  commitment of protection of the natural resources by the residents, while improving their living conditions.

(d)  Modelling Units: R   I   I     The project presents three management units: conservation MU, agroforestry MU and commercial MU  eforestation for conservation is carried out in National Parks, protected areas and their buffer zones. The  objective is aimed at the conservation of water and soil resources, through the reforestation and  conservation of the forests, thus achieving a connectivity of the same. Native species are used in a mixture  that seeks to reproduce the diversity that occurs in nature, favoring the development of biodiversity, the  gradual recovery of vegetation cover of intervened areas, the reduction of erosive processes, the  improvement of surface runoff and the control of white straw (Saccharum spontaneum).  n agroforestry and silvopastoral activities, natural resource management is promoted, integrating  communities as a fundamental part of their development, with a focus on property planning, sustainability  and protection of natural resources, providing an economic productive option for communities by  promoting agroforestry systems that also generate food security products and reducing anthropogenic  pressure on remaining forest areas.  n commercial reforestation valuable forest species are used for later harvesting for commercial timber,  providing resources to cover the initial investment and the continuity and expansion of the program.

Evidence must be given that the nature conservation area is managed in order to establish, maintain or  restore the natural ecosystem of the landscape the project is integrated in.: There is sufficient evidence provided that ACP is seeking to restore the natural landscape along the watershed.  The  project  utilizes  several  native  species  in  the  conservation  area  and  includes  a  rigorous  monitoring  and  research program in collaboration with the Smithsonian Tropical Resources Institute (STRI).  During the field visit  some MUs of Soberania National Park  and Ciudad del árbol were inspected.  These sites showed elements of  initial  secondary  forest  succession  such  as:  1)  pioneer  tree  species  detected,  which  were  not  planted  by  the  project, but were natural regeneration; 2) soil composition by the presence of forest litter showed the recovery  of  the   ecosystem.  According   to  the  interview  held  with  the  technical  expert,  wildlife  animals’   footprints   were   spotted by the workers, which help to spread the seeds from the adjacent rainforest to the conservation MUs  proposed.   The  audit  team  has  confirmed  through  interviews,  field  observations  and  document  review  that  the  nature  conservation area is managed for natural ecosystem restoration. | :

C AR#:  29/16: Findings for Evaluation of  Evidence: | : T   T The  project  has  updated  the  Sustainability  Monitoring  Plan  to  reflect  the  mitigation  measures  outlined in the completed Do-No-Harm Assessment template.  he vast majority of the project area was without protective vegetation in the baseline scenario,  and  the  planting  area  was  100%  overrun  by  the  introduced  and  invasive  weed  Saccharum  spontaneum  in the  conservation  and  commercial  reforestation  areas.  The  soils  of  commercial  areas were also covered by Saccharum spontaneum.  The agroforestry areas did not meet the  definition  of  forest,  but  had  lower  presence  of  Saccharum  spontaneum  nonetheless.  Ciri  Grande  area  has  soils  of  low  productive  capacity.  The  use  of  land  was  not  in  sync  with  the  land’s natural ability, and was generating negative impacts on the productive base and negative  externalities  such  as  erosion  and  loss  of  biodiversity.    The  updated  Sustainability  Monitoring  Plan  outlines  how  biodiversity  and  socioeconomic  improvement  will  be  monitored,  and  these  are discussed at greater length in the project’s various management plans.   he CAR is closed.

T   a   b 1.  The baseline is the ‘woody biomass’ and ‘non-woody biomass’ on the eligible planting area just before the  planting start.  he calculation can be done in two different ways:  .   By executing field measurements. Here,  the  ‘Forest  Inventory’  guideline shall be applied.  .   By estimating the biomass in reference to similar areas  (cid:120)  regional and national default values shall preferably be used  (cid:120)  international default values can only be used if other values are not available: C   T   T The developer has resolved the issue the  CAR was based  on, having chosen a  different value for the baseline  carbon stocks, which is described below in the findings for Corrective Action Request 09-02.  Therefore the issue  of citing the dry matter value incorrectly as carbon, has been resolved.    CAR closed  orrective  Action  Request  09-02:  ACP  shall  include  remnant  trees  besides  the  tropical  moist  grassland,  to  determine baseline carbon stocks in the management units.  Feedback project developer (09 September 2013):  The developer has chosen a different method for estimating baseline carbon stocks which is more conservative  and does adequately address the concern that remnant and isolated trees were not accurately quantified in the  original baseline carbon stock estimate, which was based on grassland stocks.  The new carbon stock values for  the  baseline  are  different  for  commercial/conservation  management  units  and  for  agroforestry/silvopasture  management units.    he new value for commercial/conservation management units is 23tCO2/ha.  The new value for silvopasture and  agroforestry management units is 31tCO2/ha.  Conclusion of the certifier (27. September 2013):  The revised biomass carbon stock for commercial and conservation areas is derived from an academic paper based  on field measurements in Colombia.  This paper cites biomass stocks 8 tdm/ha.  This PDD reports the value as for  total  dry  biomass,  including  above  and  belowground  pools  (8  *1.58  to  estimate  belowground  biomass).   Converted to CO2 the carbon stocks is 23tCO2/ha.  The audit team has reviewed the PDF output of the carbon  calculations  on  the  Climate  Projects  registry  (File:  Management-Units  ACP  CFS.pdf)  and  confirmed  that  the  baseline  carbon  values  have  been  updated.    The  audit  team  has  additionally  reviewed  the  FOFI  model  and  confirmed that this has been updated as well.  The new carbon values are in conformance with the standard in  that they are calculated without some of the errors observed during the field audit (See CAR 09-01), are based on  scientific  literature,  and  are  calculated  correctly.    The  audit  team  observed  during  the  field  audit  that  the  commercial  and  conservation  areas  had  minimal  remnant  trees  from  before  project  establishment  and  were  mostly  saccharum  grass.    The  revised  baseline  stocks  are  acceptable  for  commercial  and  conservation  management units.     he revised biomass carbon stock for silvopasture and agroforestry systems is derived from an academic paper  also based on field measurements in Colombia of pasture systems with remnant trees.  This paper cites biomass  stocks as 17.2 tdm/ha.  This value is also for total dry biomass, including above and belowground pools.  Converted  to CO2 the value becomes 31tCO2/ha.   The audit team has reviewed the PDF output of the carbon calculations  on the Climate Projects registry (File: Management-Units ACP CFS.pdf) and confirmed that the baseline carbon  values have been updated.  The audit team has additionally reviewed the FOFI model and Baseline section of the  PD and confirmed that this has been updated as well.  The revised value of 31tCO2/ha for the silvopasture and  agroforestry management units is acceptable and in conformance with the standard in that it reflects the site  conditions  observed  during  the  field  audit  in  which  remnant  trees  from  before  the  project  start  date,  were  observed.    The  audit  team  noted  considerable  variability  between  different  sites  within  the  agroforestry  and  silvopasture management units, with some sites having no remnant trees and others having several.  This value  from analogous locations in a scientific paper is acceptable.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the GS+Certification+Report+16.12.2013.pdf (Page 139) indicates that at least 30.49% of the eligible planting area (1770 ha out of 2985 ha) is managed as a nature conservation area, which meets the requirement of Criterion 4. This satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement as it directly measures the percentage of forest areas under sustainable management practices. The evidence from the PDD_ACP_CFS.pdf (Page 3) and GS+Project+Information+09.27.2017.pdf (Page 7) also supports the implementation of specific management practices such as selective logging, reforestation, and certification schemes. However, the outcomes of these management practices in terms of forest health, biodiversity, and ecosystem services are described qualitatively, and the key stakeholders involved are mentioned but not explicitly quantified. The policies, laws, or regulations in place to support and enforce sustainable forest management practices are also mentioned but not explicitly quantified. Therefore, the overall score is 2.

1.  A description of the project’s general forest management objectives must be given.:  | : For all the 4 activities of the project land preparation will be a mix:  Manually or mechanically, depending on the topography of the place. To  prevent soil erosion. The land used to implement the reforestation project was before covered with  Saccharum spontaneum or degraded  soil that was used for catting. In both cases it is necessary ploughing to give the soil the right texture before planting. In the case of  catting  areas the soil is compacted and there is a higher impermeability. Ploughing is employed in flat areas and avoided in areas with slopes. This  is a security principle to avoid post erosion from ploughing. No slash and burn technics are used for land preparation.  ( Ref.03-02, page 1, points a,b,)(Ref.03-04, page 1, point a,b,c)

1.  A description of the project’s general forest management objectives must be given.: g. Thinning | : W Thinning  is  an  important  objective  for  all  timber  tress  and  especially  applied  in  the  commercial  and  agroforestry  activity.    The  main  aim  is  to  improve the yield after the end of the period.  ithin of the planned management schemes to manage the different forestry schemes are established the thinning activity, in order to:  (i)increased  light  penetration  to  develop  bigger  crowns  to  accelerate  diameter  growth,  (ii)increased  percentage  of  trees  reaching  maturity  improved wood quality, (iii)encouraged root development control of ground cover vegetation for erosion control,  (iv)improve the tree distance  and to maximize the growth.   The trees selected for thinning shall be selected to maximize the volume and value of the trees left for later thinning or felling, when larger piece  size prices will be higher. A sanitary thinning will be executed followed by a selective thinning and roughly 50% of the original trees removed in  these  thinning  by  year  five.  Non-commercial  trees  will  be  left  on  the  ground  for  nutrient  release  to  the  soil,  support  fauna  and  flora,  thus  increasing total biodiversity. (Ref. 01-04, page 130, paragraph 1)

A description of the project’s general forest management objectives must be given.: The PDD states the general objectives of forest management, describing the different activities that are taking  place during the development of the project.  However, the forest management objectives differ dependingon  the  MU  planting  activity  planting  activity  (commercial,  agroforestry,  silvopasture  or  conservation  objectives).  These objectives are not well described in this section. (FAR 03-01)  In the field, the audit team evaluated some of the activities presented in this section, such as land preparation,  and planting. There are some inconsistencies with the data provided on the PDD, such as spacing for silvopastoral  systems, which in the field were observed at 20x20m while the PDD states 10x10m.  The nursery was not visited but the technical advisor of the project described a nursery ran by the women of the  surrounding of Chagres National Park. Women collect the seeds from the forest  for the native species. On the  other hand, the farmers from Capira sector that participate in the project received the seedling from contractors,  there were no local nurseries established yet.  According to the description of activities for commercial plots which will comprise thinning and pruning and a  final  cut  at  25  years,  it  will  not  correspond  to  the  Selective  Harvesting  method  as  mentioned  in  the  PD.    The  rotation  harvesting  approach  will  be  used.    During  the  visit,  a  technical  advisor  in  charge  of  the  agroforestry  systems mentioned that timber species in the farms will be harvested upon farmer’s convenience.  They expressed  that about 20% of the trees will be harvested. (FAR 03-01) | :

An average maintenance of the plantation of 4 times a year was established.  There are several activities that are part of this maintenance. For agroforestry, silvopasture and commercial forestry activities:  Cleaning the area  around the tress with a radius of 50 centimeters and applying selective organic fertilization known as bocashi are important activities to consider.   or all activities: Protecting the reforestation area from grazing animals is achieved by surrounding the area with a living fence, using barbwire  and special tree species as fence posts such as Poro (Erythrina poeppigiana). 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the total above and below ground biomass stock in forests within a specific geographic scope. The document GS+Certification+Report+16.12.2013.pdf mentions biomass stocks for commercial and conservation areas (23tCO2/ha) and agroforestry and silvopasture management units (31tCO2/ha), but these values are not explicitly stated in tonnes of dry biomass (tdm). The belowground biomass stock is estimated to be 12.64 tdm/ha, but this is not directly measured for the specified geographic scope. Therefore, the evidence is treated conservatively, and the score is 1.
